# Plano de Mitigação — CloudFront 5XX em concertacaoamazonia.com.br (2026-04-20, 12:08 BRT)

## Contexto

O BIT Monitoring (rule_id 10 — "CloudFront 5XX Rate Critical", threshold 10%, duração 600s)
disparou às 12:08 BRT de **2026-04-20** com valor 14.67%. Apesar do alerta mencionar "CloudFront 5XX",
a investigação mostrou que a causa não é o CloudFront nem o WordPress retornando 5XX de aplicação —
é **saturação de CPU no origin** causando timeouts que o CloudFront contabiliza como 5XX.

### Evidências coletadas (2026-04-20 14:21 BRT)

**No EC2 (i-059febbd720286cd4, t3.large, 2 vCPUs, 8GB RAM):**
- `uptime`: load average **18.22, 18.04, 18.02** — 9x a capacidade (load "saudável" ≤ 2.0).
- `top`: 95.7% us CPU, 0% idle. Memória OK (2.6GB usados + 5GB buff/cache, 4GB swap livre).
- **19 workers PHP-FPM em estado R (running) simultâneos**, cada um com TIME+ de 37–42 min — workers
  "grudados" em queries lentas, não saindo do pool por 40+ minutos.
- FPM atual: `pm.max_children = 18`, `request_terminate_timeout = 120s`, `pm.max_requests = 500`.

**No nginx error log (/var/log/nginx/error.log):**
- Múltiplos `upstream timed out (110: Connection timed out) while reading response header from upstream`,
  todos em `GET /en/wp-json/tribe/events/v1/events/64545` (TEC REST API).
- Probes de diretório em `/wp-includes/blocks/*`, `/wp-includes/Requests/`, `/wp-content/uploads/` —
  scan de reconnaissance de bots.

**No access log (últimos 30min):**
- **434 de 490 requests (88%) terminaram em 499** (client closed connection = CloudFront desistiu do origin).
- User-Agents dominantes: Amazon CloudFront (413), **Bytespider (50)**, Baiduspider (13+6),
  "Mozilla compatible; crawler" (12), YandexImages (6), PetalBot (3).
- URLs mais batidas pelo crawler 64.252.79.99: `/en/eventos-calendario/2025-03-08/`,
  `/en/eventos-calendario/list/page/17/?tribe-bar-date=2023-08-02&eventDisplay=past`,
  `/eventos/lista/?jet_download=...&eventDisplay=past` — **exatamente o padrão TEC crawler trap
  documentado em `feedback_tec_crawler_traps.md`.**

**Métricas ALB (sa-east-1):**
- `HTTPCode_Target_5XX_Count` = 3 no período (aplicação não está retornando 5XX).
- `HTTPCode_ELB_5XX_Count` = 0.
- CloudFront vê 5xxErrorRate 14.67% porque o CloudFront contabiliza timeouts/origin unreachable
  como 5XX, mesmo quando o ALB não registrou resposta 5XX (conexões abandonadas = 499 no nginx,
  nada no ALB, 504 no CloudFront).

### Por que quebrou desta vez

A mitigação documentada em `feedback_tec_crawler_traps.md` (2026-04-08) tinha **4 camadas**:
1. `cleaned_args` strip no nginx → ✅ **presente** (`/etc/nginx/nginx.conf` tem o map)
2. `$rocket_skip_reason` usando `$cleaned_args` → ✅ **presente**
3. `robots.txt` com `Disallow: /*?ical=1`, `/*?tribe-bar-date=`, `/*?eventDate=` → ❌ **AUSENTE**
   - `curl https://concertacaoamazonia.com.br/robots.txt` retorna apenas o default WP
     (`Disallow: /wp-admin/`). As regras TEC nunca chegaram à produção ou foram perdidas.
4. FPM `max_children` ≈ 5/vCPU → ⚠️ **degradado** (18 em 2 vCPUs = 9/vCPU)

Resultado: crawlers (Bytespider em particular) descobriram URLs TEC com
`?tribe-bar-date=` e `?eventDisplay=past`, o CloudFront forward tudo pro origin (cache miss garantido
por QS), 18 workers FPM ficam presos em queries `usort()` sobre `tribe_events` + TEC Month View,
cada um rodando por até 120s (request_terminate_timeout). Demais requests legítimos caem em fila,
CloudFront timeout, 499 no nginx, 5XX no CloudFront.

## Objetivo

1. **Estancar o incidente agora** (próximos minutos): libertar workers FPM e bloquear crawlers
   no edge, sem exigir deploy.
2. **Impedir recidiva** (próximas horas/dias): reinstalar as defesas documentadas (robots.txt,
   FPM tune) e adicionar barreiras complementares no CloudFront.
3. **Visibilidade** (próximo ciclo): criar alertas de load + 499 para detectar antes de explodir.

## Etapa 1 — Mitigação imediata (≤ 5 min, reversível)

### 1.1 Reciclar o pool FPM para libertar workers presos
```bash
ssh concertacaoamazonia.com.br-prod-sa "sudo systemctl reload php8.3-fpm && uptime"
```
- `reload` (não `restart`) termina workers que ultrapassaram `request_terminate_timeout`
  e spawna novos. Sem downtime.
- Verificar load 60s depois. Esperado: queda de 18 → < 4 em minutos após os novos workers
  servirem páginas cacheadas pelo WP Rocket / CloudFront.

### 1.2 Instalar `robots.txt` com Disallows TEC
- Arquivo alvo: `/var/www/concertacaoamazonia.com.br/robots.txt` (nginx serve estático).
- Conteúdo mínimo (baseado no memory `feedback_tec_crawler_traps.md`):
  ```
  User-agent: *
  Disallow: /wp-admin/
  Disallow: /*?ical=1
  Disallow: /*?outlook-ical=1
  Disallow: /*?tribe-bar-date=
  Disallow: /*?eventDate=
  Disallow: /*?eventDisplay=past
  Disallow: /*?jet_download=
  Allow: /wp-admin/admin-ajax.php

  Sitemap: https://concertacaoamazonia.com.br/wp-sitemap.xml
  ```
- Crawlers sérios (Googlebot, Bingbot) respeitam. Bytespider/Baidu respeitam parcialmente —
  é necessária camada 1.3 abaixo.

### 1.3 Bloquear Bytespider/Baidu/Yandex/PetalBot no WAF existente

**Estado atual do WAF** (confirmado via `wafv2 get-web-acl`):
- Distribuição `E2F1QD7E7YOYEB` já tem WebACL `ACL-WPAdminHML` associada (scope CLOUDFRONT, us-east-1).
- ALB `amazonia-alb` já tem WebACL `amazonia-waf` associada (scope REGIONAL, sa-east-1).
- Rule `Block-AggressiveBots` (prio 6) na CloudFront WebACL já bloqueia: `TikTokSpider`, `GPTBot`,
  `DataForSeoBot`, `AhrefsBot`, `SemrushBot`, `MJ12bot` (ByteMatchStatement em OrStatement).
- Rule `RateLimit-300-Block` (prio 7): 300 req / 5min por IP — não pega os bots atuais porque o
  CloudFront distribui o tráfego por várias edge IPs (cada IP fica abaixo do limite).
- **CloudWatch últimas 3h: 0 blocks em todas as rules de bot.** Os atores de hoje (Bytespider,
  Baiduspider, YandexImages, PetalBot) **não estão na lista**.

**Ação**: acrescentar 4 novos `ByteMatchStatement` ao `OrStatement` da rule `Block-AggressiveBots`
existente. Sem criar rule nova, sem CloudFront Function, sem custo adicional.

User-Agents a adicionar (comparação CONTAINS em header `user-agent`):
```
Bytespider
Baiduspider
YandexImages
PetalBot
```

Como aplicar (via AWS CLI, sem interface):
```bash
# 1. Capturar WebACL atual (LockToken obrigatório para UpdateWebACL)
aws --profile Concertação wafv2 get-web-acl \
  --name ACL-WPAdminHML --scope CLOUDFRONT \
  --id 05522267-513d-4346-8e56-ba18b11e950b --region us-east-1 > /tmp/webacl.json

# 2. Gerar payload com 4 novos ByteMatch em Block-AggressiveBots.OrStatement.Statements[]
#    (script auxiliar editando o JSON — manter os 6 UAs existentes, acrescentar os 4 novos)

# 3. Aplicar:
aws --profile Concertação wafv2 update-web-acl \
  --name ACL-WPAdminHML --scope CLOUDFRONT \
  --id 05522267-513d-4346-8e56-ba18b11e950b \
  --region us-east-1 \
  --lock-token "<token do get-web-acl>" \
  --default-action Allow={} \
  --visibility-config <manter config atual> \
  --rules file:///tmp/rules-updated.json
```

**Propagação**: mudanças no WAF CloudFront propagam em ~1–3 min para todos os edges.

**Reversível**: reaplicar `update-web-acl` com a lista anterior (backup do JSON).

## Etapa 2 — Ajustes estruturais (mesma janela, ainda ≤ 30 min)

### 2.1 FPM tuning: reduzir `max_children`
Memória sugere 10 workers (5/vCPU). Pool atual tem 18 — **ajustar para 10** e aumentar
ritmo de reciclagem:
```bash
# Arquivo: /etc/php/8.3/fpm/pool.d/wordpress.conf
pm.max_children = 10
pm.max_requests = 200           # era 500 — recicla mais rápido (libera memória, evita leaks)
request_terminate_timeout = 30s # era 120s — mata worker preso rápido
pm.start_servers = 4
pm.min_spare_servers = 2
pm.max_spare_servers = 6
```
- Depois: `sudo systemctl reload php8.3-fpm`.
- Razão do 10: com WordPress + WPML + JetEngine + TEC, cada request pesado pode usar 1 vCPU
  durante ~0.5s; com 2 vCPUs só conseguimos servir ~4 dinâmicos/s simultaneamente. 10 workers
  com `max_requests=200` + `terminate=30s` dá margem para pico sem destruir CPU.

### 2.2 nginx fastcgi timeouts — encurtar para falhar rápido
`fastcgi_read_timeout 60;` é o que está permitindo um worker segurar uma conexão por 60s enquanto
o CloudFront desiste em ~30s (499 no nginx). Ajustar para **20s** no default do site (manter 15s
nos paths já configurados). Worker termina, libera slot para request real.
- Arquivo: `/etc/nginx/sites-available/concertacaoamazonia.com.br`.
- Rodar `sudo nginx -t && sudo systemctl reload nginx`.

### 2.3 Atualizar `$cleaned_args` map (defesa preventiva)
Confirmar que o map em `/etc/nginx/nginx.conf` inclui todos estes tokens (se algum falta, acrescentar):
```
tribe-bar-date  eventDate  eventDisplay=past  ical  outlook-ical  jet_download
gclid  fbclid  utm_source  utm_medium  utm_campaign  utm_term  utm_content  nowprocket
```
Já há map — revisar e completar. Isso aumenta hit-rate do WP Rocket cache e do CloudFront
(menos variantes por URL). Nota: `jet_download` precisa continuar **forwardado** para o origin
via origin request policy (`WP-Dynamic-ForwardHostQS` já faz isso), mas **removido da cache key**
(`WP-Dynamic-ShortTTL-NoQS` com whitelist já faz isso).

### 2.4 Versionar `bit-tec-cache.php` e sincronizar com `common/mu-plugins/`
O mu-plugin está modificado no repo local mas a cópia canônica em
`/Users/dcambria/scripts/server-tools/v2/docker-dev/common/mu-plugins/bit-tec-cache.php`
precisa ser atualizada e commitada. Não é causa raiz do incidente atual (TEC cache cobre
só `tribe_events_is_new_install`), mas está no plano de higiene.

## Etapa 3 — Prevenção (próximas 24h)

### 3.1 Alertar ANTES do 5XX — novos alarmes
Adicionar 3 rules no BIT Monitoring (análogos às existentes em
`/Users/dcambria/scripts/bit-monitoring/backend/scripts/create_cloudwatch_alert_rules.py`):
- **EC2 CPU > 90% por 5min** → warning.
- **EC2 load-avg > 4 por 5min** (via CloudWatch Agent custom metric) → warning.
- **CloudFront `4xx+5xx ratio` > 20% ou 499 spike em nginx** → warning (captura timeouts
  que não aparecem como 5XX).

### 3.2 Documentar o incidente
Criar `docs/incidents/2026-04-20-tec-crawler-cloudfront-5xx.md` com:
- timeline (12:08 alerta → 14:21 diagnóstico → mitigação aplicada)
- causa raiz: robots.txt sem regras TEC + FPM `max_children=18`
- ação tomada e checklist para evitar regressão em próximos deploys

### 3.3 Reinstalar robots.txt no deploy
O script `03-nginx-sites.sh` (ou o responsável por gerar `/var/www/<site>/robots.txt`)
deve **sempre** criar o arquivo com os Disallows TEC para este site — caso contrário um próximo
blue-green deploy vai recriar o problema. Verificar se a mitigação foi adicionada ao script
após o incidente de 2026-04-08.

## Arquivos e paths críticos

| Caminho | O que fazer |
|---|---|
| `/etc/php/8.3/fpm/pool.d/wordpress.conf` (prod) | Reduzir `max_children` 18 → 10, `request_terminate_timeout` 120s → 30s, `max_requests` 500 → 200 |
| `/etc/nginx/sites-available/concertacaoamazonia.com.br` (prod) | `fastcgi_read_timeout` 60 → 20 |
| `/etc/nginx/nginx.conf` (prod) | Revisar map `$cleaned_args` — completar tokens se faltar |
| `/var/www/concertacaoamazonia.com.br/robots.txt` (prod) | Criar com Disallows TEC + jet_download |
| WAF `ACL-WPAdminHML` (CloudFront, us-east-1) | Acrescentar Bytespider/Baiduspider/YandexImages/PetalBot à rule `Block-AggressiveBots` existente |
| `docker-dev/common/mu-plugins/bit-tec-cache.php` (repo) | Atualizar com a versão de `sites/concertacao/wordpress/wp-content/mu-plugins/bit-tec-cache.php` e commitar |
| `server-tools/v2/ec2-deploy/scripts/03-nginx-sites.sh` | Adicionar geração de robots.txt com regras TEC no deploy |

## Verificação

Depois de cada etapa, confirmar em ordem:

1. **Etapa 1.1 (FPM reload):**
   ```bash
   ssh concertacaoamazonia.com.br-prod-sa "uptime && ps aux | grep 'php-fpm' | grep -v grep | wc -l"
   ```
   Esperado: load decrescente, workers ≤ 10.

2. **Etapa 1.2 (robots.txt):**
   ```bash
   curl -s https://concertacaoamazonia.com.br/robots.txt | grep -c 'tribe-bar-date'
   ```
   Esperado: 1 ou mais.

3. **Etapa 1.3 (WAF rule update):**
   ```bash
   # Aguardar ~2min pela propagação do WAF, depois:
   curl -sI -A 'Bytespider' https://concertacaoamazonia.com.br/eventos/ | head -1
   # E confirmar no CloudWatch:
   aws --profile Concertação cloudwatch get-metric-statistics \
     --namespace AWS/WAFV2 --metric-name BlockedRequests \
     --dimensions Name=WebACL,Value=ACL-WPAdminHML Name=Rule,Value=Block-AggressiveBots Name=Region,Value=CloudFront \
     --start-time $(date -u -d '10 min ago' +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ) \
     --end-time $(date -u +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ) \
     --period 60 --statistics Sum --region us-east-1
   ```
   Esperado: `HTTP/2 403` no curl + `BlockedRequests > 0` no CloudWatch.

4. **Etapa 2.1/2.2 (FPM + nginx):**
   ```bash
   ssh concertacaoamazonia.com.br-prod-sa "sudo grep max_children /etc/php/8.3/fpm/pool.d/*.conf && sudo grep fastcgi_read_timeout /etc/nginx/sites-available/concertacaoamazonia.com.br"
   ```

5. **Métricas CloudFront 15min depois das mitigações:**
   ```bash
   aws --profile Concertação cloudwatch get-metric-statistics \
     --namespace AWS/CloudFront --metric-name 5xxErrorRate \
     --dimensions Name=DistributionId,Value=E2F1QD7E7YOYEB Name=Region,Value=Global \
     --start-time $(date -u -d '15 min ago' +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ) \
     --end-time $(date -u +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ) \
     --period 60 --statistics Average Maximum --region us-east-1
   ```
   Esperado: `Average < 1%`.

6. **Validação visual via Playwright** (se desejado): navegar home + `/en/eventos-calendario/` e
   confirmar carregamento sem timeout, tempo de resposta < 2s.

## Risco / reversibilidade

- **1.1 (FPM reload):** zero risco — operação rotineira, restaura em segundos.
- **1.2 (robots.txt):** baixo risco — crawlers bem-comportados (Google) não são afetados
  (continuam indexando tudo exceto URLs TEC com QS). Reversível: deletar o arquivo.
- **1.3 (WAF update):** **baixo** — acrescentamos 4 ByteMatchStatement à rule existente
  `Block-AggressiveBots` que já bloqueia 6 outros bots em prod há semanas sem incidente.
  Reversível em <1 min (reaplicar JSON anterior via `update-web-acl`). Sem custo adicional.
- **2.1 (FPM max_children 18→10):** **médio** — se tráfego real (não-crawler) subir muito, pode
  gerar fila. Mitigado: com crawler bloqueado no edge, carga real é muito menor que a medida agora.
- **2.2 (fastcgi_read_timeout 60→20):** baixo — 20s é suficiente para 99% das queries legítimas
  (as que demoram >20s já estavam contribuindo para o problema). Cautela em endpoints admin
  — manter o `fastcgi_read_timeout` separado para `wp-admin` se já existir.

---

## Decisões confirmadas com o usuário

1. **Bloqueio de bots**: reutilizar a rule WAF `Block-AggressiveBots` existente na WebACL
   `ACL-WPAdminHML` (CloudFront). Sem criar CloudFront Function ou regra nova.
2. **FPM tuning**: **aplicar agora**, junto da Etapa 1. `reload` (sem downtime).
3. **User-Agents a acrescentar ao `OrStatement` de `Block-AggressiveBots`**:
   - `Bytespider`
   - `Baiduspider` (cobre também `Baiduspider-render` via CONTAINS)
   - `YandexImages`
   - `PetalBot`

skills.name: superpowers:executing-plans
skills.content:
Base directory for this skill: /Users/dcambria/.claude/plugins/cache/claude-plugins-official/superpowers/5.0.7/skills/executing-plans

# Executing Plans

## Overview

Load plan, review critically, execute all tasks, report when complete.

**Announce at start:** "I'm using the executing-plans skill to implement this plan."

**Note:** Tell your human partner that Superpowers works much better with access to subagents. The quality of its work will be significantly higher if run on a platform with subagent support (such as Claude Code or Codex). If subagents are available, use superpowers:subagent-driven-development instead of this skill.

## The Process

### Step 1: Load and Review Plan
1. Read plan file
2. Review critically - identify any questions or concerns about the plan
3. If concerns: Raise them with your human partner before starting
4. If no concerns: Create TodoWrite and proceed

### Step 2: Execute Tasks

For each task:
1. Mark as in_progress
2. Follow each step exactly (plan has bite-sized steps)
3. Run verifications as specified
4. Mark as completed

### Step 3: Complete Development

After all tasks complete and verified:
- Announce: "I'm using the finishing-a-development-branch skill to complete this work."
- **REQUIRED SUB-SKILL:** Use superpowers:finishing-a-development-branch
- Follow that skill to verify tests, present options, execute choice

## When to Stop and Ask for Help

**STOP executing immediately when:**
- Hit a blocker (missing dependency, test fails, instruction unclear)
- Plan has critical gaps preventing starting
- You don't understand an instruction
- Verification fails repeatedly

**Ask for clarification rather than guessing.**

## When to Revisit Earlier Steps

**Return to Review (Step 1) when:**
- Partner updates the plan based on your feedback
- Fundamental approach needs rethinking

**Don't force through blockers** - stop and ask.

## Remember
- Review plan critically first
- Follow plan steps exactly
- Don't skip verifications
- Reference skills when plan says to
- Stop when blocked, don't guess
- Never start implementation on main/master branch without explicit user consent

## Integration

**Required workflow skills:**
- **superpowers:using-git-worktrees** - REQUIRED: Set up isolated workspace before starting
- **superpowers:writing-plans** - Creates the plan this skill executes
- **superpowers:finishing-a-development-branch** - Complete development after all tasks

content:
<EXTREMELY_IMPORTANT>
You have superpowers.

**Below is the full content of your 'superpowers:using-superpowers' skill - your introduction to using skills. For all other skills, use the 'Skill' tool:**

---
name: using-superpowers
description: Use when starting any conversation - establishes how to find and use skills, requiring Skill tool invocation before ANY response including clarifying questions
---

<SUBAGENT-STOP>
If you were dispatched as a subagent to execute a specific task, skip this skill.
</SUBAGENT-STOP>

<EXTREMELY-IMPORTANT>
If you think there is even a 1% chance a skill might apply to what you are doing, you ABSOLUTELY MUST invoke the skill.

IF A SKILL APPLIES TO YOUR TASK, YOU DO NOT HAVE A CHOICE. YOU MUST USE IT.

This is not negotiable. This is not optional. You cannot rationalize your way out of this.
</EXTREMELY-IMPORTANT>

## Instruction Priority

Superpowers skills override default system prompt behavior, but **user instructions always take precedence**:

1. **User's explicit instructions** (CLAUDE.md, GEMINI.md, AGENTS.md, direct requests) — highest priority
2. **Superpowers skills** — override default system behavior where they conflict
3. **Default system prompt** — lowest priority

If CLAUDE.md, GEMINI.md, or AGENTS.md says "don't use TDD" and a skill says "always use TDD," follow the user's instructions. The user is in control.

## How to Access Skills

**In Claude Code:** Use the `Skill` tool. When you invoke a skill, its content is loaded and presented to you—follow it directly. Never use the Read tool on skill files.

**In Copilot CLI:** Use the `skill` tool. Skills are auto-discovered from installed plugins. The `skill` tool works the same as Claude Code's `Skill` tool.

**In Gemini CLI:** Skills activate via the `activate_skill` tool. Gemini loads skill metadata at session start and activates the full content on demand.

**In other environments:** Check your platform's documentation for how skills are loaded.

## Platform Adaptation

Skills use Claude Code tool names. Non-CC platforms: see `references/copilot-tools.md` (Copilot CLI), `references/codex-tools.md` (Codex) for tool equivalents. Gemini CLI users get the tool mapping loaded automatically via GEMINI.md.

# Using Skills

## The Rule

**Invoke relevant or requested skills BEFORE any response or action.** Even a 1% chance a skill might apply means that you should invoke the skill to check. If an invoked skill turns out to be wrong for the situation, you don't need to use it.

```dot
digraph skill_flow {
    "User message received" [shape=doublecircle];
    "About to EnterPlanMode?" [shape=doublecircle];
    "Already brainstormed?" [shape=diamond];
    "Invoke brainstorming skill" [shape=box];
    "Might any skill apply?" [shape=diamond];
    "Invoke Skill tool" [shape=box];
    "Announce: 'Using [skill] to [purpose]'" [shape=box];
    "Has checklist?" [shape=diamond];
    "Create TodoWrite todo per item" [shape=box];
    "Follow skill exactly" [shape=box];
    "Respond (including clarifications)" [shape=doublecircle];

    "About to EnterPlanMode?" -> "Already brainstormed?";
    "Already brainstormed?" -> "Invoke brainstorming skill" [label="no"];
    "Already brainstormed?" -> "Might any skill apply?" [label="yes"];
    "Invoke brainstorming skill" -> "Might any skill apply?";

    "User message received" -> "Might any skill apply?";
    "Might any skill apply?" -> "Invoke Skill tool" [label="yes, even 1%"];
    "Might any skill apply?" -> "Respond (including clarifications)" [label="definitely not"];
    "Invoke Skill tool" -> "Announce: 'Using [skill] to [purpose]'";
    "Announce: 'Using [skill] to [purpose]'" -> "Has checklist?";
    "Has checklist?" -> "Create TodoWrite todo per item" [label="yes"];
    "Has checklist?" -> "Follow skill exactly" [label="no"];
    "Create TodoWrite todo per item" -> "Follow skill exactly";
}
```

## Red Flags

These thoughts mean STOP—you're rationalizing:

| Thought | Reality |
|---------|---------|
| "This is just a simple question" | Questions are tasks. Check for skills. |
| "I need more context first" | Skill check comes BEFORE clarifying questions. |
| "Let me explore the codebase first" | Skills tell you HOW to explore. Check first. |
| "I can check git/files quickly" | Files lack conversation context. Check for skills. |
| "Let me gather information first" | Skills tell you HOW to gather information. |
| "This doesn't need a formal skill" | If a skill exists, use it. |
| "I remember this skill" | Skills evolve. Read current version. |
| "This doesn't count as a task" | Action = task. Check for skills. |
| "The skill is overkill" | Simple things become complex. Use it. |
| "I'll just do this one thing first" | Check BEFORE doing anything. |
| "This feels productive" | Undisciplined action wastes time. Skills prevent this. |
| "I know what that means" | Knowing the concept ≠ using the skill. Invoke it. |

## Skill Priority

When multiple skills could apply, use this order:

1. **Process skills first** (brainstorming, debugging) - these determine HOW to approach the task
2. **Implementation skills second** (frontend-design, mcp-builder) - these guide execution

"Let's build X" → brainstorming first, then implementation skills.
"Fix this bug" → debugging first, then domain-specific skills.

## Skill Types

**Rigid** (TDD, debugging): Follow exactly. Don't adapt away discipline.

**Flexible** (patterns): Adapt principles to context.

The skill itself tells you which.

## User Instructions

Instructions say WHAT, not HOW. "Add X" or "Fix Y" doesn't mean skip workflows.


</EXTREMELY_IMPORTANT>

"""
Create CloudWatch alert rules and notification rule.

Based on AWS best practices, community standards (CloudPosse, Blue Matador, marbot),
and the existing cloudwatch-metrics-audit.md.

Run inside backend container:
  docker exec bit-backend python scripts/create_cloudwatch_alert_rules.py
"""
from app.core.sync_database import SyncSessionLocal
from app.models.alert_rule import AlertRule
from app.models.notification_rule import NotificationRule, TriggerType

db = SyncSessionLocal()

# ─── 1. CloudWatch Notification Rule ─────────────────────────────────
# Mirrors Host Metric Alert — same channels and severity filter.
# Dynamically looks up Host Metric Alert to copy its channel_ids
# (avoids hardcoding IDs that differ between dev and prod).

existing = db.query(NotificationRule).filter(
    NotificationRule.trigger_type == TriggerType.CLOUDWATCH_METRIC_THRESHOLD
).first()

if existing:
    print(f"CloudWatch notification rule already exists: [{existing.id}] {existing.name}")
else:
    # Copy channels from Host Metric Alert (same routing pattern)
    host_rule = db.query(NotificationRule).filter(
        NotificationRule.trigger_type == TriggerType.HOST_METRIC_THRESHOLD
    ).first()
    channel_ids = host_rule.channel_ids if host_rule else []

    if not channel_ids:
        print("WARNING: No Host Metric Alert rule found — creating CloudWatch rule without channels")

    nr = NotificationRule(
        name="CloudWatch Metric Alert",
        description="Alert when CloudWatch metrics exceed thresholds (RDS, ALB, CloudFront) or CloudWatch agent goes offline",
        trigger_type=TriggerType.CLOUDWATCH_METRIC_THRESHOLD,
        severity_filter=["critical", "warning"],
        channel_ids=channel_ids,
        cooldown_minutes=15,
        include_details=True,
        is_active=True,
    )
    db.add(nr)
    db.flush()
    print(f"Created notification rule: [{nr.id}] {nr.name} (channels: {channel_ids})")

# ─── 2. CloudWatch Threshold AlertRules ───────────────────────────────
# Duration: 600s (10 min) minimum for CloudWatch (2 data points)
# Cooldown: 300s (5 min) matching existing host rules

RULES = [
    # ── RDS ──────────────────────────────────────────────
    {
        "name": "RDS CPU High",
        "description": "RDS CPU utilization exceeds 70%",
        "resource_type": "rds",
        "metric": "aws.rds.cpu_utilization",
        "operator": ">",
        "threshold": "70",
        "duration": 600,
        "severity": "warning",
    },
    {
        "name": "RDS CPU Critical",
        "description": "RDS CPU utilization exceeds 90%",
        "resource_type": "rds",
        "metric": "aws.rds.cpu_utilization",
        "operator": ">",
        "threshold": "90",
        "duration": 600,
        "severity": "critical",
    },
    {
        "name": "RDS Low Memory",
        "description": "RDS freeable memory below 256 MB",
        "resource_type": "rds",
        "metric": "aws.rds.freeable_memory",
        "operator": "<",
        "threshold": "268435456",  # 256 MB in bytes
        "duration": 600,
        "severity": "warning",
    },
    {
        "name": "RDS Memory Critical",
        "description": "RDS freeable memory below 64 MB — swap imminent",
        "resource_type": "rds",
        "metric": "aws.rds.freeable_memory",
        "operator": "<",
        "threshold": "67108864",  # 64 MB in bytes
        "duration": 600,
        "severity": "critical",
    },
    {
        "name": "RDS Low Storage",
        "description": "RDS free storage space below 5 GB",
        "resource_type": "rds",
        "metric": "aws.rds.free_storage_space",
        "operator": "<",
        "threshold": "5368709120",  # 5 GB in bytes
        "duration": 600,
        "severity": "warning",
    },
    {
        "name": "RDS Storage Critical",
        "description": "RDS free storage space below 2 GB — write failures imminent",
        "resource_type": "rds",
        "metric": "aws.rds.free_storage_space",
        "operator": "<",
        "threshold": "2147483648",  # 2 GB in bytes
        "duration": 600,
        "severity": "critical",
    },
    {
        "name": "RDS Read Latency High",
        "description": "RDS read latency exceeds 10ms",
        "resource_type": "rds",
        "metric": "aws.rds.read_latency",
        "operator": ">",
        "threshold": "0.01",  # 10ms in seconds
        "duration": 600,
        "severity": "warning",
    },
    {
        "name": "RDS Read Latency Critical",
        "description": "RDS read latency exceeds 20ms — I/O bottleneck",
        "resource_type": "rds",
        "metric": "aws.rds.read_latency",
        "operator": ">",
        "threshold": "0.02",  # 20ms in seconds
        "duration": 600,
        "severity": "critical",
    },
    {
        "name": "RDS Write Latency High",
        "description": "RDS write latency exceeds 10ms",
        "resource_type": "rds",
        "metric": "aws.rds.write_latency",
        "operator": ">",
        "threshold": "0.01",  # 10ms in seconds
        "duration": 600,
        "severity": "warning",
    },
    {
        "name": "RDS Write Latency Critical",

        "metric": "aws.rds.write_latency",
        "operator": ">",
        "threshold": "0.02",  # 20ms in seconds

        "threshold": "134217728",  # 128 MB in bytes
        "duration": 600,
        "severity": "warning",
    },
    {
        "name": "RDS Swap Usage Critical",
        "description": "RDS swap usage exceeds 256 MB — severe performance degradation",
        "resource_type": "rds",
        "metric": "aws.rds.swap_usage",
        "operator": ">",
        "threshold": "268435456",  # 256 MB in bytes
        "duration": 600,
        "severity": "critical",
    },
    {
        "name": "RDS Disk Queue High",
        "description": "RDS disk queue depth exceeds 32 — I/O backlog building",
        "resource_type": "rds",
        "metric": "aws.rds.disk_queue_depth",
        "operator": ">",
        "threshold": "32",
        "duration": 600,
        "severity": "warning",
    },
    {
        "name": "RDS Disk Queue Critical",
        "description": "RDS disk queue depth exceeds 64 — storage cannot keep up",
        "resource_type": "rds",
        "metric": "aws.rds.disk_queue_depth",
        "operator": ">",
        "threshold": "64",
        "duration": 600,
        "severity": "critical",
    },

    # ── ALB ──────────────────────────────────────────────
    {
        "name": "ALB Unhealthy Hosts",
        "description": "ALB has unhealthy targets — traffic not reaching backends",
        "resource_type": "alb",
        "metric": "aws.alb.unhealthy_hosts",
        "operator": ">",
        "threshold": "0",
        "duration": 600,
        "severity": "critical",
    },
    {
        "name": "ALB Target 5XX High",
        "description": "Backend returning more than 10 server errors per 5 min",
        "resource_type": "alb",
        "metric": "aws.alb.http_5xx_count",
        "operator": ">",
        "threshold": "10",
        "duration": 600,
        "severity": "warning",
    },
    {
        "name": "ALB Target 5XX Critical",
        "description": "Backend returning more than 50 server errors per 5 min — major outage",
        "resource_type": "alb",
        "metric": "aws.alb.http_5xx_count",
        "operator": ">",
        "threshold": "50",
        "duration": 600,
        "severity": "critical",
    },
    {
        "name": "ALB ELB 5XX High",
        "description": "ALB itself returning 5XX errors — targets unreachable",
        "resource_type": "alb",
        "metric": "aws.alb.elb_5xx_count",
        "operator": ">",
        "threshold": "5",
        "duration": 600,
        "severity": "warning",
    },
    {
        "name": "ALB ELB 5XX Critical",
        "description": "ALB returning many 5XX errors — all targets likely down",
        "resource_type": "alb",
        "metric": "aws.alb.elb_5xx_count",
        "operator": ">",
        "threshold": "25",
        "duration": 600,
        "severity": "critical",
    },
    {
        "name": "ALB Response Time High",
        "description": "Target response time exceeds 2 seconds",
        "resource_type": "alb",
        "metric": "aws.alb.target_response_time",
        "operator": ">",
        "threshold": "2",
        "duration": 600,
        "severity": "warning",
    },
    {
        "name": "ALB Response Time Critical",
        "description": "Target response time exceeds 5 seconds — user experience severely degraded",
        "resource_type": "alb",

    {
        "name": "ALB Rejected Connections",

    {

    },

    # ── CloudFront ───────────────────────────────────────
    {
        "name": "CloudFront 5XX Rate High",

        "resource_type": "cloudfront",
        "metric": "aws.cloudfront.5xx_error_rate",
        "operator": ">",
        "threshold": "1",
        "duration": 600,
        "severity": "warning",
    },

created = 0
skipped = 0

for rule_data in RULES:
    # Check if rule with same name already exists
    existing = db.query(AlertRule).filter(AlertRule.name == rule_data["name"]).first()
    if existing:
        print(f"  SKIP (exists): [{existing.id}] {existing.name}")
        skipped += 1
        continue

    rule = AlertRule(
        name=rule_data["name"],
        description=rule_data["description"],
        resource_type=rule_data["resource_type"],
        metric=rule_data["metric"],
        operator=rule_data["operator"],
        threshold=rule_data["threshold"],
        duration=rule_data["duration"],
        severity=rule_data["severity"],
        cooldown=300,
        is_active=True,
    )
    db.add(rule)
    db.flush()
    print(f"  CREATED: [{rule.id}] {rule.name} | {rule.resource_type} {rule.metric} {rule.operator} {rule.threshold} ({rule.severity})")
    created += 1

db.commit()
db.close()

print(f"\nDone! Created {created} rules, skipped {skipped} (already existed)")
print(f"Total CloudWatch threshold rules: {created + skipped}")

"""CloudWatch metrics polling service.

Polls AWS CloudWatch for metrics from managed services (RDS, ALB, CloudFront)
and stores them in the InfraMetric hypertable via virtual OtelAgent entries.

This module handles:
- Metric definitions per resource type
- Virtual agent lifecycle (create/deactivate)
- Batched CloudWatch GetMetricData API calls
- Metric normalization and storage
"""

import hashlib
import logging
import secrets
from datetime import datetime, timedelta, timezone
from typing import Optional

from sqlalchemy import select
from sqlalchemy.orm import Session

from app.models.cloud_account import CloudAccount
from app.models.infra_metric import InfraMetric
from app.models.otel_agent import OtelAgent
from app.models.resource import Resource, ResourceType
from app.providers.aws.client import AWSClientFactory

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)


# Metric definitions per resource type
# Each metric maps to a CloudWatch metric with its namespace, stat, and dimension key
CLOUDWATCH_METRICS = {
    ResourceType.RDS: {
        "namespace": "AWS/RDS",
        "dimension_key": "DBInstanceIdentifier",
        "metrics": [
            {"name": "CPUUtilization", "stat": "Average", "normalized": "aws.rds.cpu_utilization", "unit": "%"},
            {"name": "FreeableMemory", "stat": "Average", "normalized": "aws.rds.freeable_memory", "unit": "By"},
            {"name": "DatabaseConnections", "stat": "Average", "normalized": "aws.rds.database_connections", "unit": "1"},
            {"name": "ReadLatency", "stat": "Average", "normalized": "aws.rds.read_latency", "unit": "s"},
            {"name": "WriteLatency", "stat": "Average", "normalized": "aws.rds.write_latency", "unit": "s"},
            {"name": "FreeStorageSpace", "stat": "Average", "normalized": "aws.rds.free_storage_space", "unit": "By"},
            {"name": "ReadIOPS", "stat": "Average", "normalized": "aws.rds.read_iops", "unit": "1/s"},
            {"name": "WriteIOPS", "stat": "Average", "normalized": "aws.rds.write_iops", "unit": "1/s"},
            {"name": "SwapUsage", "stat": "Average", "normalized": "aws.rds.swap_usage", "unit": "By"},
            {"name": "DiskQueueDepth", "stat": "Average", "normalized": "aws.rds.disk_queue_depth", "unit": "1"},
        ],
    },
    ResourceType.ALB: {
        "namespace": "AWS/ApplicationELB",
        "dimension_key": "LoadBalancer",
        "metrics": [
            {"name": "RequestCount", "stat": "Sum", "normalized": "aws.alb.request_count", "unit": "1"},
            {"name": "TargetResponseTime", "stat": "Average", "normalized": "aws.alb.target_response_time", "unit": "s"},
            {"name": "HTTPCode_Target_5XX_Count", "stat": "Sum", "normalized": "aws.alb.http_5xx_count", "unit": "1"},
            {"name": "HTTPCode_ELB_5XX_Count", "stat": "Sum", "normalized": "aws.alb.elb_5xx_count", "unit": "1"},
            {"name": "ActiveConnectionCount", "stat": "Average", "normalized": "aws.alb.active_connections", "unit": "1"},
            {"name": "RejectedConnectionCount", "stat": "Sum", "normalized": "aws.alb.rejected_connections", "unit": "1"},
        ],
    },
    ResourceType.CLOUDFRONT: {
        "namespace": "AWS/CloudFront",
        "dimension_key": "DistributionId",
        "metrics": [
            {"name": "Requests", "stat": "Sum", "normalized": "aws.cloudfront.requests", "unit": "1"},
            {"name": "BytesDownloaded", "stat": "Sum", "normalized": "aws.cloudfront.bytes_downloaded", "unit": "By"},
            {"name": "4xxErrorRate", "stat": "Average", "normalized": "aws.cloudfront.4xx_error_rate", "unit": "%"},
            {"name": "5xxErrorRate", "stat": "Average", "normalized": "aws.cloudfront.5xx_error_rate", "unit": "%"},
        ],
    },
}

# ALB metrics that require both LoadBalancer + TargetGroup dimensions
ALB_TARGET_GROUP_METRICS = [
    {"name": "UnHealthyHostCount", "stat": "Maximum", "normalized": "aws.alb.unhealthy_hosts", "unit": "1"},
    {"name": "HealthyHostCount", "stat": "Minimum", "normalized": "aws.alb.healthy_hosts", "unit": "1"},
    {"name": "TargetConnectionErrorCount", "stat": "Sum", "normalized": "aws.alb.target_connection_errors", "unit": "1"},
]

# Resource types we poll CloudWatch for
CLOUDWATCH_RESOURCE_TYPES = [ResourceType.RDS, ResourceType.ALB, ResourceType.CLOUDFRONT]


def ensure_virtual_agent(account: CloudAccount, db: Session) -> OtelAgent:
    """Create or reactivate a virtual OtelAgent for a cloud account's CloudWatch metrics.

    Virtual agents allow CloudWatch metrics to use the same InfraMetric table
    (which requires agent_id) and appear naturally on the infra dashboard.

    Returns:
        The virtual OtelAgent instance.
    """
    # Check for existing virtual agent for this account
    existing = db.execute(
        select(OtelAgent).where(
            OtelAgent.cloud_account_id == account.id,
            OtelAgent.agent_type == "cloudwatch",
        )
    ).scalar_one_or_none()

    if existing:
        if not existing.is_active:
            existing.is_active = True
            logger.info(f"Reactivated virtual CloudWatch agent {existing.id} for account {account.name}")
        return existing

    # Create new virtual agent with a dummy token hash (not used for auth)
    token_hash = hashlib.sha256(secrets.token_hex(32).encode()).hexdigest()

    agent = OtelAgent(
        name=f"CloudWatch - {account.name}",
        hostname=f"cloudwatch.{account.aws_account_id or account.id}.amazonaws.com",
        token_hash=token_hash,
        agent_type="cloudwatch",
        os_type="cloudwatch",
        agent_version="cloudwatch-poller-1.0",
        cloud_account_id=account.id,
        monitoring_client_id=account.monitoring_client_id,
        is_active=True,
        heartbeat_failure_threshold=3,  # 3 missed polls = 15 min before alerting
    )
    db.add(agent)
    db.flush()  # Get the ID assigned
    logger.info(f"Created virtual CloudWatch agent {agent.id} for account {account.name}")
    return agent


def deactivate_virtual_agent(account: CloudAccount, db: Session) -> None:
    """Deactivate the virtual agent when CloudWatch is disabled for an account."""
    agent = db.execute(
        select(OtelAgent).where(
            OtelAgent.cloud_account_id == account.id,
            OtelAgent.agent_type == "cloudwatch",
        )
    ).scalar_one_or_none()

    if agent and agent.is_active:
        agent.is_active = False
        logger.info(f"Deactivated virtual CloudWatch agent {agent.id} for account {account.name}")


def _get_alb_arn(resource: Resource) -> Optional[str]:
    """Get full ALB ARN from resource metadata."""
    metadata = resource.resource_metadata or {}
    return metadata.get("load_balancer_arn") or metadata.get("arn")


def _get_alb_dimension_value(resource: Resource) -> Optional[str]:
    """Extract ALB dimension value from resource metadata.

82	def _format_value(metric: str, value: float) -> str:
83	    """Format a metric value with human-readable units for notifications."""
84	    if metric in BYTE_METRICS:
85	        abs_val = abs(value)
86	        if abs_val >= 1_073_741_824:
87	            return f"{value / 1_073_741_824:.1f} GB"
88	        if abs_val >= 1_048_576:
89	            return f"{value / 1_048_576:.0f} MB"
90	        if abs_val >= 1_024:
91	            return f"{value / 1_024:.0f} KB"
92	        return f"{value:.0f} B"
93	    if metric in SECOND_METRICS:
94	        if abs(value) < 1:
95	            return f"{value * 1000:.1f} ms"
96	        return f"{value:.2f} s"
97	    if metric in PERCENT_01_METRICS:
98	        return f"{value * 100:.1f}%"
99	    if metric in PERCENT_100_METRICS:

"""Infrastructure metric alert evaluation tasks.

Periodically evaluates AlertRule thresholds against InfraMetric data
and triggers alerts via SyncAlertService. Also checks for stale agents.
"""

import logging
from datetime import datetime, timedelta, timezone

from sqlalchemy import select, func, and_

from app.core.sync_database import sync_db_session
from app.models.alert_rule import AlertRule
from app.models.incident import Incident, IncidentStatus
from app.models.infra_metric import InfraMetric
from app.models.otel_agent import OtelAgent
from app.models.in_app_notification import NotificationType
from app.models.notification_rule import TriggerType, Severity
from app.services.alert_service_sync import SyncAlertService
from workers.celery_app import celery_app

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)

# Map metric names to human-readable descriptions
METRIC_LABELS = {
    "system.cpu.utilization": "CPU Usage",
    "system.memory.utilization": "Memory Usage",
    "system.filesystem.utilization": "Disk Usage",
    "container.cpu.utilization": "Container CPU",
    "container.memory.percent": "Container Memory",
    # CloudWatch RDS metrics
    "aws.rds.cpu_utilization": "RDS CPU",
    "aws.rds.freeable_memory": "RDS Freeable Memory",
    "aws.rds.database_connections": "RDS Connections",
    "aws.rds.read_latency": "RDS Read Latency",
    "aws.rds.write_latency": "RDS Write Latency",
    "aws.rds.free_storage_space": "RDS Free Storage",
    "aws.rds.read_iops": "RDS Read IOPS",
    "aws.rds.write_iops": "RDS Write IOPS",
    "aws.rds.swap_usage": "RDS Swap Usage",
    "aws.rds.disk_queue_depth": "RDS Disk Queue Depth",
    # CloudWatch ALB metrics
    "aws.alb.request_count": "ALB Request Count",
    "aws.alb.target_response_time": "ALB Response Time",
    "aws.alb.http_5xx_count": "ALB Target 5XX Errors",
    "aws.alb.elb_5xx_count": "ALB ELB 5XX Errors",
    "aws.alb.active_connections": "ALB Active Connections",
    "aws.alb.rejected_connections": "ALB Rejected Connections",
    "aws.alb.unhealthy_hosts": "ALB Unhealthy Hosts",
    "aws.alb.healthy_hosts": "ALB Healthy Hosts",
    "aws.alb.target_connection_errors": "ALB Target Connection Errors",
    # CloudWatch CloudFront metrics
    "aws.cloudfront.requests": "CloudFront Requests",
    "aws.cloudfront.bytes_downloaded": "CloudFront Bytes Downloaded",
    "aws.cloudfront.4xx_error_rate": "CloudFront 4XX Rate",
    "aws.cloudfront.5xx_error_rate": "CloudFront 5XX Rate",
}

# CloudWatch resource types
CLOUDWATCH_RESOURCE_TYPES = ["rds", "alb", "cloudfront"]

# Metric unit types for human-readable formatting in notifications
BYTE_METRICS = {
    "aws.rds.freeable_memory", "aws.rds.free_storage_space",
    "aws.rds.swap_usage", "aws.cloudfront.bytes_downloaded",
}
SECOND_METRICS = {
    "aws.rds.read_latency", "aws.rds.write_latency",
    "aws.alb.target_response_time",
}
PERCENT_01_METRICS = {
    "system.cpu.utilization", "system.memory.utilization",
    "system.filesystem.utilization",
}
PERCENT_100_METRICS = {
    "aws.rds.cpu_utilization", "aws.cloudfront.4xx_error_rate",
    "aws.cloudfront.5xx_error_rate", "container.cpu.utilization",
    "container.memory.percent",
}


def _format_value(metric: str, value: float) -> str:
    """Format a metric value with human-readable units for notifications."""
    if metric in BYTE_METRICS:
        abs_val = abs(value)
        if abs_val >= 1_073_741_824:
            return f"{value / 1_073_741_824:.1f} GB"
        if abs_val >= 1_048_576:
            return f"{value / 1_048_576:.0f} MB"
        if abs_val >= 1_024:
            return f"{value / 1_024:.0f} KB"
        return f"{value:.0f} B"
    if metric in SECOND_METRICS:
        if abs(value) < 1:
            return f"{value * 1000:.1f} ms"
        return f"{value:.2f} s"
    if metric in PERCENT_01_METRICS:
        return f"{value * 100:.1f}%"
    if metric in PERCENT_100_METRICS:
        return f"{value:.1f}%"
    # Count-based metrics: clean number
    if value == int(value):
        return f"{int(value)}"
    return f"{value:.1f}"


def _find_open_incident(db, trigger_type_value: str, resource_type: str, resource_id: str, metric: str):
    """Find an existing open/acknowledged incident for the same agent+metric."""
    incidents = db.execute(
        select(Incident).where(
            and_(
                Incident.status.in_([IncidentStatus.OPEN, IncidentStatus.ACKNOWLEDGED]),
                Incident.trigger_type == trigger_type_value,
                Incident.resource_type == resource_type,
                Incident.resource_id == resource_id,
            )
        )
    ).scalars().all()

    # Filter by metric name in details JSON

input.content:
# 2026-04-20 — CloudFront 5XX em concertacaoamazonia.com.br por crawlers TEC

**Severidade:** Critical
**Alerta:** BIT Monitoring rule_id 10 ("CloudFront 5XX Rate Critical", threshold 10%, duration 600s)
**Valor no pico:** 14.67% de 5xxErrorRate
**Duração:** ~2h13 (12:08 → 14:21 BRT)
**Autor:** Daniel Cambría

---

## Timeline

| Horário (BRT) | Evento |
|---|---|
| 12:08 | Alerta disparado (rule_id 10). Thiago Canani notifica. |
| 14:15 | Inicio do diagnóstico via SSH no origin (i-059febbd720286cd4). |
| 14:21 | Causa raiz identificada. Plano de mitigação montado. |
| 14:30 | Etapa 1.1 — `systemctl reload php8.3-fpm` (reciclagem de workers). |
| 14:32 | Etapa 1.2 — `robots.txt` físico criado com Disallows TEC. |
| 14:35 | Etapa 1.3 — WAF `Block-AggressiveBots` estendida com 4 UAs (Bytespider, Baiduspider, YandexImages, PetalBot). |
| 14:40 | Etapa 2.1 — FPM tuning: `max_children` 18→10, `request_terminate_timeout` 120s→30s, `max_requests` 500→200. |
| 14:42 | Etapa 2.2 — nginx `fastcgi_read_timeout` 60→20s (default public PHP location apenas). |
| 14:45 | Etapa 2.3 — map `$cleaned_args` acrescentado token `jet_download`. |
| 14:50 | Load médio do origin: 0.18 (antes: 18.22). CloudFront 5xxErrorRate: 0.00%. 499s no nginx no período: 0 (antes: 434 em 30 min). |

## Causa raiz

**Saturação de CPU no origin** causada por 19 workers PHP-FPM presos processando queries
lentas de `usort()` sobre `tribe_events` (TEC Month View / Week View), disparadas por
crawlers (Bytespider dominante, seguido de Baiduspider, YandexImages, PetalBot) batendo
em URLs com query strings TEC que burlavam o cache:

- `?tribe-bar-date=YYYY-MM-DD`
- `?eventDate=YYYY-MM-DD`
- `?eventDisplay=past`
- `?jet_download=...`

O CloudFront contabiliza origin timeouts como 5XX (mesmo quando a aplicação nunca retorna
um 5XX real — ALB `HTTPCode_Target_5XX_Count` = 3 no período, CloudFront reportou 14.67%).

### Por que quebrou desta vez

Das 4 camadas de defesa documentadas em memory `feedback_tec_crawler_traps.md`
(incidente original 2026-04-08):

| Camada | Estado em 2026-04-20 |
|---|---|
| 1. `$cleaned_args` strip no nginx | ✅ presente |
| 2. `$rocket_skip_reason` usando `$cleaned_args` | ✅ presente |
| 3. `robots.txt` com Disallows TEC | ❌ **AUSENTE** — WP gerava virtualmente, apenas `Disallow: /wp-admin/` |
| 4. FPM `max_children` ≈ 5/vCPU | ⚠️ **DEGRADADO** — 18 em 2 vCPUs = 9/vCPU |

Crawlers respeitam parcialmente robots.txt; mesmo com robots, alguns (Bytespider, Baidu)
exigem bloqueio explícito no WAF — que também estava faltando.

## Ações tomadas (permanentes)

1. **robots.txt físico** — `/var/www/concertacaoamazonia.com.br/robots.txt` criado com
   Disallows TEC + `jet_download`. Invalidação CloudFront: `I5VM2LX8DDH4UYYL06SF4ASV7I`.
2. **WAF `ACL-WPAdminHML` / rule `Block-AggressiveBots`** — acrescentados 4 ByteMatchStatement
   ao OrStatement existente: `Bytespider`, `Baiduspider`, `YandexImages`, `PetalBot`.
   UAs que já estavam bloqueados (preservados): GPTBot, DataForSeoBot, AhrefsBot, SemrushBot, MJ12bot.
3. **PHP-FPM pool** (`/etc/php/8.3/fpm/pool.d/wordpress.conf`):
   - `pm.max_children`: 18 → 10
   - `pm.start_servers`: 6 → 4
   - `pm.min_spare_servers`: 3 → 2
   - `pm.max_spare_servers`: 10 → 6
   - `pm.max_requests`: 500 → 200 (recicla mais rápido, libera memória)
   - `request_terminate_timeout`: 120s → 30s (mata worker preso rápido)
4. **nginx** (`/etc/nginx/sites-available/concertacaoamazonia.com.br`):
   - Location PHP default: `fastcgi_read_timeout` 60 → 20s, `fastcgi_send_timeout` 60 → 20s
   - Mantidos: wp-admin 120s, async-upload 300s.
5. **nginx map `$cleaned_args`** (`/etc/nginx/nginx.conf`): token `jet_download` acrescentado
   (já tinha `tribe-bar-date`, `eventDate`, `eventDisplay=past`, `ical`, `outlook-ical`, etc.)

Todos os arquivos têm backup `.bak-2026-04-20`.

## Métricas antes/depois

| Métrica | Antes | Depois |
|---|---|---|
| Load avg (1 min) | 18.22 | 0.18 |

- `CloudFront 4XX Rate High` — warning @ 5% (captura tempestades de crawler antes do 5XX)

toolUseResult.content:
# 2026-04-20 — CloudFront 5XX em concertacaoamazonia.com.br por crawlers TEC

**Severidade:** Critical
**Alerta:** BIT Monitoring rule_id 10 ("CloudFront 5XX Rate Critical", threshold 10%, duration 600s)
**Valor no pico:** 14.67% de 5xxErrorRate
**Duração:** ~2h13 (12:08 → 14:21 BRT)
**Autor:** Daniel Cambría

---

## Timeline

| Horário (BRT) | Evento |
|---|---|
| 12:08 | Alerta disparado (rule_id 10). Thiago Canani notifica. |
| 14:15 | Inicio do diagnóstico via SSH no origin (i-059febbd720286cd4). |
| 14:21 | Causa raiz identificada. Plano de mitigação montado. |
| 14:30 | Etapa 1.1 — `systemctl reload php8.3-fpm` (reciclagem de workers). |
| 14:32 | Etapa 1.2 — `robots.txt` físico criado com Disallows TEC. |
| 14:35 | Etapa 1.3 — WAF `Block-AggressiveBots` estendida com 4 UAs (Bytespider, Baiduspider, YandexImages, PetalBot). |
| 14:40 | Etapa 2.1 — FPM tuning: `max_children` 18→10, `request_terminate_timeout` 120s→30s, `max_requests` 500→200. |
| 14:42 | Etapa 2.2 — nginx `fastcgi_read_timeout` 60→20s (default public PHP location apenas). |
| 14:45 | Etapa 2.3 — map `$cleaned_args` acrescentado token `jet_download`. |
| 14:50 | Load médio do origin: 0.18 (antes: 18.22). CloudFront 5xxErrorRate: 0.00%. 499s no nginx no período: 0 (antes: 434 em 30 min). |

## Causa raiz

**Saturação de CPU no origin** causada por 19 workers PHP-FPM presos processando queries
lentas de `usort()` sobre `tribe_events` (TEC Month View / Week View), disparadas por
crawlers (Bytespider dominante, seguido de Baiduspider, YandexImages, PetalBot) batendo
em URLs com query strings TEC que burlavam o cache:

- `?tribe-bar-date=YYYY-MM-DD`
- `?eventDate=YYYY-MM-DD`
- `?eventDisplay=past`
- `?jet_download=...`

O CloudFront contabiliza origin timeouts como 5XX (mesmo quando a aplicação nunca retorna
um 5XX real — ALB `HTTPCode_Target_5XX_Count` = 3 no período, CloudFront reportou 14.67%).

### Por que quebrou desta vez

Das 4 camadas de defesa documentadas em memory `feedback_tec_crawler_traps.md`
(incidente original 2026-04-08):

| Camada | Estado em 2026-04-20 |
|---|---|
| 1. `$cleaned_args` strip no nginx | ✅ presente |
| 2. `$rocket_skip_reason` usando `$cleaned_args` | ✅ presente |
| 3. `robots.txt` com Disallows TEC | ❌ **AUSENTE** — WP gerava virtualmente, apenas `Disallow: /wp-admin/` |
| 4. FPM `max_children` ≈ 5/vCPU | ⚠️ **DEGRADADO** — 18 em 2 vCPUs = 9/vCPU |

Crawlers respeitam parcialmente robots.txt; mesmo com robots, alguns (Bytespider, Baidu)
exigem bloqueio explícito no WAF — que também estava faltando.

## Ações tomadas (permanentes)

1. **robots.txt físico** — `/var/www/concertacaoamazonia.com.br/robots.txt` criado com
   Disallows TEC + `jet_download`. Invalidação CloudFront: `I5VM2LX8DDH4UYYL06SF4ASV7I`.
2. **WAF `ACL-WPAdminHML` / rule `Block-AggressiveBots`** — acrescentados 4 ByteMatchStatement
   ao OrStatement existente: `Bytespider`, `Baiduspider`, `YandexImages`, `PetalBot`.
   UAs que já estavam bloqueados (preservados): GPTBot, DataForSeoBot, AhrefsBot, SemrushBot, MJ12bot.
3. **PHP-FPM pool** (`/etc/php/8.3/fpm/pool.d/wordpress.conf`):
   - `pm.max_children`: 18 → 10
   - `pm.start_servers`: 6 → 4
   - `pm.min_spare_servers`: 3 → 2
   - `pm.max_spare_servers`: 10 → 6
   - `pm.max_requests`: 500 → 200 (recicla mais rápido, libera memória)
   - `request_terminate_timeout`: 120s → 30s (mata worker preso rápido)
4. **nginx** (`/etc/nginx/sites-available/concertacaoamazonia.com.br`):
   - Location PHP default: `fastcgi_read_timeout` 60 → 20s, `fastcgi_send_timeout` 60 → 20s
   - Mantidos: wp-admin 120s, async-upload 300s.
5. **nginx map `$cleaned_args`** (`/etc/nginx/nginx.conf`): token `jet_download` acrescentado
   (já tinha `tribe-bar-date`, `eventDate`, `eventDisplay=past`, `ical`, `outlook-ical`, etc.)

Todos os arquivos têm backup `.bak-2026-04-20`.

## Métricas antes/depois

| Métrica | Antes | Depois |
|---|---|---|
| Load avg (1 min) | 18.22 | 0.18 |
| FPM workers em estado R | 19 (presos 37-42 min) | 5 (healthy) |
| 499 no nginx / 30 min | 434 | 0 |
| CloudFront 5xxErrorRate | 14.67% | 0.00% |
| curl -A Bytespider | HTTP/2 301 | HTTP/2 403 (WAF) |
| Browser normal | — | HTTP/2 301 (sem alteração) |

## Prevenção (pós-incidente)

### 1. Novos alertas no BIT Monitoring

Acrescentados em `bit-monitoring/backend/scripts/create_cloudwatch_alert_rules.py`:

- `CloudFront 4XX Rate High` — warning @ 5% (captura tempestades de crawler antes do 5XX)
- `CloudFront 4XX Rate Critical` — critical @ 20%
- `EC2 CPU High` — warning @ 80%, 10 min
- `EC2 CPU Critical` — critical @ 90%, 10 min

Load-avg não foi adicionado: a métrica ainda não é coletada pelo OTel Collector atual
(requer `hostmetrics` receiver com scraper `load`). Fica como débito técnico separado.

### 2. robots.txt como parte do deploy

TODO: atualizar `server-tools/v2/ec2-deploy/scripts/03-nginx-sites.sh` (ou script adjacente)
para gerar `robots.txt` com Disallows TEC no blue-green deploy. Sem isso, o próximo cutover
recria o problema.

### 3. Decisões confirmadas para manter

- **WP Rocket nginx bypass:** manter ativo (nginx serve HTML direto do disco).
- **FPM sizing:** ~5 workers por vCPU no t3.large (2 vCPUs → 10). Se subir a instância

content.text:
Excellent — the script already has robots.txt generation (v1.8.0, lines 465-499) but it's missing two of the current Disallows: `jet_download` and `eventDisplay=past` not strict enough. Let me cross-check against the physical file we created today, then harden.

Current script has:
- `Disallow: /eventos/????-??-??/` ✓
- `Disallow: /*?tribe-bar-date=` ✓
- `Disallow: /*?eventDate=` ✓
- `Disallow: /*?eventDisplay=past` ✓
- `Disallow: /*?ical=` ✓
- `Disallow: /*?outlook-ical=` ✓

Missing from script vs. today's physical file:
- `Disallow: /*?jet_download=`
- Explicit `Disallow: /wp-admin/` + `Allow: /wp-admin/admin-ajax.php`
- Sitemap URL uses `/sitemap_index.xml` but physical uses `/wp-sitemap.xml`

Also the condition `[[ ! -f "${WP_ROOT}/robots.txt" ]]` means existing files aren't updated. I'll bump to v1.9.0 and make it idempotent — update content if needed, plus add jet_download and wp-admin rules.

input.old_string:
if [[ "${ENVIRONMENT}" == "prod" ]] && [[ ! -f "${WP_ROOT}/robots.txt" ]]; then
    log_info "Criando robots.txt estático em ${WP_ROOT}..."
    cat > "${WP_ROOT}/robots.txt" << 'ROBOTSEOF'
User-agent: *
Allow: /

# TEC crawler traps — superfície infinita de URLs
Disallow: /eventos/????-??-??/
Disallow: /*?tribe-bar-date=
Disallow: /*?eventDate=
Disallow: /*?eventDisplay=past
Disallow: /*?ical=
Disallow: /*?outlook-ical=

# TEC REST API — custoso, sem uso público
Disallow: /wp-json/tribe/events/
Disallow: /wp-json/tribe_events/

Sitemap: https://FQDN_PLACEHOLDER/sitemap_index.xml
ROBOTSEOF
    # Substituir placeholder pelo FQDN real (heredoc quoted não expande variáveis)
    sed -i "s|FQDN_PLACEHOLDER|${FQDN}|" "${WP_ROOT}/robots.txt"
    chown "${WEB_USER}:${WEB_USER}" "${WP_ROOT}/robots.txt"
    log_success "robots.txt criado em ${WP_ROOT}/robots.txt"
else
    if [[ "${ENVIRONMENT}" == "prod" ]]; then
        log_info "robots.txt já existe em ${WP_ROOT}/robots.txt — não sobrescreve."
    else
        log_info "robots.txt não criado (ambiente ${ENVIRONMENT} — dev/hml usam X-Robots-Tag)."
    fi
fi
input.new_string:
if [[ "${ENVIRONMENT}" == "prod" ]]; then
    ROBOTS_PATH="${WP_ROOT}/robots.txt"
    # Marcador canonico usado para detectar versoes anteriores deste script
    # (incidente 2026-04-20: versoes antigas/ausentes permitiram crawler storm)
    ROBOTS_MARKER="# bit-robots v1.9.0"

    write_robots=true
    if [[ -f "$ROBOTS_PATH" ]]; then
        if grep -q "$ROBOTS_MARKER" "$ROBOTS_PATH" 2>/dev/null; then
            write_robots=false
            log_info "robots.txt ja na versao atual (${ROBOTS_MARKER}) — preservado."
        else
            log_info "robots.txt existente nao corresponde a versao atual — atualizando (backup salvo)..."
            cp "$ROBOTS_PATH" "${ROBOTS_PATH}.bak-$(date +%Y%m%d-%H%M%S)" 2>/dev/null || true
        fi
    fi

    if [[ "$write_robots" == "true" ]]; then
        log_info "Gravando robots.txt em ${ROBOTS_PATH}..."
        cat > "$ROBOTS_PATH" << 'ROBOTSEOF'
# bit-robots v1.9.0
# Gerado por server-tools/ec2-deploy/post-deploy/03-nginx-sites.sh
# NAO editar manualmente sem atualizar o marcador acima (sera sobrescrito no proximo deploy).

User-agent: *
Disallow: /wp-admin/
Allow: /wp-admin/admin-ajax.php

# TEC crawler traps — superficie infinita de URLs que saturam FPM
Disallow: /eventos/????-??-??/
Disallow: /*?tribe-bar-date=
Disallow: /*?eventDate=
Disallow: /*?eventDisplay=past
Disallow: /*?ical=
Disallow: /*?outlook-ical=

# JetEngine download endpoint (cache bypass QS) — sem uso publico para bots
Disallow: /*?jet_download=

# TEC REST API — custoso, sem uso publico
Disallow: /wp-json/tribe/events/
Disallow: /wp-json/tribe_events/

Sitemap: https://FQDN_PLACEHOLDER/wp-sitemap.xml
ROBOTSEOF
        # Substituir placeholder pelo FQDN real (heredoc quoted nao expande variaveis)
        sed -i "s|FQDN_PLACEHOLDER|${FQDN}|" "$ROBOTS_PATH"
        chown "${WEB_USER}:${WEB_USER}" "$ROBOTS_PATH"
        chmod 644 "$ROBOTS_PATH"
        log_success "robots.txt gravado em ${ROBOTS_PATH}"
    fi
else
    log_info "robots.txt nao criado (ambiente ${ENVIRONMENT} — dev/hml usam X-Robots-Tag)."
fi

            cp "$ROBOTS_PATH" "${ROBOTS_PATH}.bak-$(date +%Y%m%d-%H%M%S)" 2>/dev/null || true
        fi
    fi

    if [[ "$write_robots" == "true" ]]; then
        log_info "Gravando robots.txt em ${ROBOTS_PATH}..."
        cat > "$ROBOTS_PATH" << 'ROBOTSEOF'
# bit-robots v1.9.0
# Gerado por server-tools/ec2-deploy/post-deploy/03-nginx-sites.sh
# NAO editar manualmente sem atualizar o marcador acima (sera sobrescrito no proximo deploy).

User-agent: *
Disallow: /wp-admin/
Allow: /wp-admin/admin-ajax.php

# TEC crawler traps — superficie infinita de URLs que saturam FPM
Disallow: /eventos/????-??-??/
Disallow: /*?tribe-bar-date=
Disallow: /*?eventDate=
Disallow: /*?eventDisplay=past
Disallow: /*?ical=
Disallow: /*?outlook-ical=

# JetEngine download endpoint (cache bypass QS) — sem uso publico para bots
Disallow: /*?jet_download=

# TEC REST API — custoso, sem uso publico
Disallow: /wp-json/tribe/events/
Disallow: /wp-json/tribe_events/

Sitemap: https://FQDN_PLACEHOLDER/wp-sitemap.xml
ROBOTSEOF
        # Substituir placeholder pelo FQDN real (heredoc quoted nao expande variaveis)
        sed -i "s|FQDN_PLACEHOLDER|${FQDN}|" "$ROBOTS_PATH"
        chown "${WEB_USER}:${WEB_USER}" "$ROBOTS_PATH"
        chmod 644 "$ROBOTS_PATH"
        log_success "robots.txt gravado em ${ROBOTS_PATH}"
    fi
else
    log_info "robots.txt nao criado (ambiente ${ENVIRONMENT} — dev/hml usam X-Robots-Tag)."
fi

content.text:
All tasks complete. Following the skill, next step would be `superpowers:finishing-a-development-branch`, but the work spans three repos and production — no single branch to finish. Summarizing instead.

## Resumo

**Incidente estancado e defesas reinstaladas** em `concertacaoamazonia.com.br`. CloudFront 5xxErrorRate: 14.67% → 0%, load: 18.22 → 0.18.

**Mudanças commitáveis pendentes em 3 repos:**

1. **`bit-monitoring/`** — `backend/scripts/create_cloudwatch_alert_rules.py`: +4 rules (CloudFront 4XX High/Critical, EC2 CPU High/Critical). Rodar dentro do container: `docker exec bit-backend python scripts/create_cloudwatch_alert_rules.py`.
2. **`server-tools/v2/`** — `ec2-deploy/post-deploy/03-nginx-sites.sh` bump v1.8.0 → **v1.9.0**: robots.txt idempotente com marcador, adiciona `Disallow: /*?jet_download=` e `Allow: /wp-admin/admin-ajax.php`.
3. **`docker-dev/sites/concertacao/`** — `docs/incidents/2026-04-20-tec-crawler-cloudfront-5xx.md` (novo, timeline + causa raiz + checklist pré-deploy).

**Não versionado:** `bit-tec-cache.php` já estava sincronizado entre site e `common/mu-plugins/` — não precisou de ação.

**Débito técnico anotado no incident doc:** alerta de load-avg requer acrescentar `load` scraper ao OTel Collector (fora do escopo desta mitigação). Diga `sucesso, versione!` se quiser que eu faça os commits nos três repos.

- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, use Config tool.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (triggers) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/triggers.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).

SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.

input.command:
cd /Users/dcambria/scripts/server-tools/v2 && git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
feat(deploy): robots.txt idempotente v1.9.0 — Disallow jet_download + wp-admin

Pós-incidente 2026-04-20: crawlers TEC (Bytespider, Baiduspider, YandexImages,
PetalBot) bateram em URLs com ?tribe-bar-date=, ?eventDisplay=past e ?jet_download=
até saturar FPM. Uma das 4 camadas de defesa (robots.txt físico) estava AUSENTE —
WP servia apenas "Disallow: /wp-admin/" virtualmente.

Mudanças em 03-nginx-sites.sh (v1.8.0 → v1.9.0):
- Marcador "# bit-robots v1.9.0" para tornar a geração idempotente (backup + rewrite
  se versão divergir; preserva se já está na atual)
- Acrescenta "Disallow: /*?jet_download=" (cache bypass QS sem uso público p/ bots)
- Acrescenta "Disallow: /wp-admin/" + "Allow: /wp-admin/admin-ajax.php"
- Sitemap aponta para /wp-sitemap.xml (nativo WP 5.5+) em vez de /sitemap_index.xml

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)" && git log --oneline -1

input.command:
git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
docs(incidents): post-mortem do CloudFront 5XX de 2026-04-20

Timeline, causa raiz (crawlers TEC saturando FPM via ?tribe-bar-date=,
?eventDisplay=past, ?jet_download=), ações tomadas (FPM 18→10 workers,
nginx fastcgi_read_timeout 60→20s, robots.txt físico, WAF Block-AggressiveBots
estendida com 4 UAs) e checklist pré-próximo deploy.

Métricas antes/depois: load 18.22→0.18, 5xxErrorRate 14.67%→0%, 499 em 30min 434→0.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)" && git log --oneline -1

- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, use Config tool.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (triggers) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/triggers.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.

content.text:
3 commits criados, todos locais (não pushei). Nenhum push sem autorização.

| Repo | Branch | Commit | Tipo |
|---|---|---|---|
| `bit-monitoring` | `feat/security-attack-monitoring` | `02738e2` | feat — 4 alertas CloudWatch |
| `server-tools/v2` | `main` | `f17e755cd` | feat — 03-nginx-sites.sh v1.8.0 → **v1.9.0** |
| `concertacao` (site) | `main` | `6c0814049` | docs — incidente 2026-04-20 |

**No repo do site ficaram sem stage** dezenas de arquivos modificados pré-existentes (CLAUDE.md, docker-compose.yml, mu-plugins, tema, screenshots) e untracked — nenhum deles relacionado ao incidente de hoje. Quer que eu faça `git push` dos 3 branches?
